cards.title: Dermatology — Common Skin Conditions Overview
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionKey FeaturesTriggersNaturopathic Approach
Atopic eczemaPruritic, flexural, dry skin, IgE-mediatedAllergens, stress, heat, soap, woolElimination diet, probiotics, Vit D, evening primrose oil, topical coconut oil
PsoriasisSilvery plaques, Auspitz sign, nail pittingStress, infection (strep), alcohol, NSAIDsFish oil 3–4g/day, Vit D3+K2, elimination diet, stress management, aloe vera topical
Acne vulgarisComedones, papules, pustules, nodulesHormones, dairy, high-GI foods, stressLow-GI diet, eliminate dairy, zinc 30mg, niacinamide topical, tea tree oil 5%
RosaceaFacial erythema, telangiectasia, papulopustulesSun, alcohol, spicy food, heat, stressAzelaic acid, green tea extract, avoid triggers, Vit B3, treat SIBO (associated)
UrticariaWheals, intense itch, angioedemaAllergens, aspirin/NSAIDs, infection, stressQuercetin 500mg BD, Vit C, identify and remove trigger, antihistamine herbs
Seborrhoeic dermatitisDandruff, greasy scales, scalp/nasolabialMalassezia yeast, stress, coldZinc pyrithione shampoo, selenium sulphide, probiotics, Vit B6, reduce sugar

cards.title: Acne — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Diet Modification ★ Low-glycaemic diet (most important): eliminate white bread, sugar, rice, cereals. Each unit ↑ in GL → 13% ↑ acne risk. Eliminate dairy (IGF-1 and whey protein are acnegenic). Add: low-sugar foods, omega-3 (sardines, flaxseed), colourful antioxidant vegetables.
2
Key Supplements Zinc 30mg/day (reduces 5-alpha reductase, anti-inflammatory, equals tetracycline in mild-moderate acne — RCT evidence). Niacinamide 500mg BD (sebum regulation). Vit A 10,000 IU (3 months, not in pregnancy). Fish oil 2g EPA/day.
3
Topical Treatments Tea tree oil 5% gel = equivalent to benzoyl peroxide 5% (RCT evidence). Niacinamide 4–5% cream (reduces sebum, anti-inflammatory). Aloe vera gel (anti-inflammatory, promotes healing). Manuka honey (antimicrobial, wound healing).
4
Hormonal Acne (PCOS) Spearmint tea 2 cups/day (anti-androgenic — reduces free testosterone). Chaste tree (Vitex) 400mg/day (LH/FSH regulation). Inositol 2–4g/day. Address insulin resistance with low-GI diet + exercise.
🟡 Gut-Skin Axis
54% of acne patients have significant gut dysbiosis. Treat the gut: probiotics (Lactobacillus acidophilus + Bifidobacterium), eliminate constipation (toxin reabsorption), identify food intolerances. If hormonal acne + GI symptoms + hirsutism → screen for PCOS (ultrasound, testosterone, LH:FSH ratio, fasting insulin).

cards.title: Psoriasis — Naturopathic Management
cards._legacyHtml:
InterventionEvidenceDose/ProtocolMechanism
Fish oil (EPA+DHA)★★★ Good3–4g EPA/dayCompetes with arachidonic acid, reduces LTB4 (key psoriasis mediator)
Vitamin D3★★★★ Strong4000–6000 IU/day + topical calcipotriolKeratinocyte differentiation, immune modulation
Curcumin★★★ Good1000mg with piperine BDNF-κB inhibition, TNF-α reduction
Mahonia aquifolium (Oregon grape) topical★★★ Good10% cream BDInhibits keratinocyte proliferation
Gluten-free diet★★ ModerateStrict GFD if anti-gliadin Ab+Reduces autoimmune trigger in coeliac-associated psoriasis
Dead Sea salt baths★★★ Good25% salt solution, 30 min, 3x/weekMineral absorption (Mg, Br), UVB exposure
Stress management★★★ GoodMBSR, yoga, meditationHPA axis normalisation, neurogenic inflammation ↓
🟡 Psoriasis + Metabolic Syndrome
Psoriasis is NOT just a skin disease — it is a systemic inflammatory condition. 30% have psoriatic arthritis. 40–60% have metabolic syndrome. Screen every psoriasis patient for: T2DM (HbA1c), dyslipidaemia (lipid panel), hypertension, depression, and CVD risk. Treating metabolic syndrome IMPROVES psoriasis severity independently of skin-directed treatment.

cards.title: Wound Healing — Naturopathic Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
StageDurationNaturopathic InterventionEvidence
Haemostasis0–hoursTurmeric powder (haemostatic), calendula compress, direct pressureModerate
Inflammatory1–4 daysManuka honey UMF 10+ dressing, tea tree oil 5% diluted, aloe vera gelStrong for honey
Proliferative4 days–3 weeksCalendula cream, Centella asiatica (gotu kola) cream, Vit E oilGood
Remodelling3 weeks–2 yearsSilicone gel (Grade A for hypertrophic scar), rosehip oil massage, Vit C 1g oralStrong for silicone
Diabetic ulcerChronicManuka honey dressing, PEMF, Vit C + zinc oral, refer vascular if not healing 4 weeksGood for honey
H
Manuka Honey Protocol Use UMF 10+ or MGO 263+. Apply 1–2mm layer directly to wound. Cover with non-adherent dressing. Change every 24–48 hours depending on exudate. Broad-spectrum antimicrobial including MRSA. Caution: honey allergy, diabetic patients (monitor blood glucose).
S
Scar Prevention Start when wound fully epithelialised. Silicone gel sheet 12 hrs/day × 3 months — Grade A for hypertrophic scar prevention. Rosehip seed oil BD massage — improves colour and texture. SPF 50+ over new scars for 1 year (UV darkens scars permanently).
🟡 Red Flags — Refer Urgently
Cellulitis (spreading redness, warmth, systemic fever) → IV antibiotics urgently. Diabetic foot ulcer with Wagner grade 2+ or signs of infection → vascular surgery + podiatry. Necrotising fasciitis (rapidly spreading, crepitus, severe or paradoxically painless) → EMERGENCY surgery within hours. Never delay referral for wound complications in diabetic, immunocompromised, or vascular patients.

cards.title: Hair Loss — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
TypePatternRoot CausesNaturopathic Protocol
Androgenic alopecia (M)Hamilton-Norwood recession, vertexDHT sensitivity, insulin resistanceSaw palmetto 320mg/day, zinc 30mg, biotin 5mg, pumpkin seed oil 400mg
Androgenic alopecia (F)Ludwig — central thinningPCOS, iron deficiency, menopauseCheck ferritin (target >70), TSH, androgens; spearmint tea BD, iron if deficient
Telogen effluviumDiffuse shedding 2–4 months after triggerIllness, surgery, childbirth, rapid weight loss, stressIdentify and remove trigger, ferritin >70, zinc, biotin, protein 1.2g/kg — resolves 6–9 months
Alopecia areataPatchy loss, exclamation mark hairsAutoimmune trigger, stressVit D, zinc, probiotics, stress management — refer dermatology for intralesional steroids
Seborrhoeic dermatitisFlaky oily scalp, MalasseziaDysbiosis, low zinc/B6, stress, sugarKetoconazole 2% shampoo EOD × 4 weeks, then zinc pyrithione maintenance, probiotics, B6
🟡 Ferritin Target for Hair
Lab normal for ferritin (>12 µg/L) is insufficient for hair growth. Target ferritin >70 µg/L for hair health. At ferritin <40: shedding increases significantly. Supplement: iron bisglycinate 25–50mg with Vit C 250mg, recheck in 3 months. Full hair loss workup: ferritin, B12, folate, zinc, TSH, free T4, testosterone, DHEA-S, SHBG. In women — always exclude PCOS and thyroid disease before treating hair loss symptomatically.

cards.title: Eczema & Fungal Infections — Naturopathic Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
ATOPIC ECZEMA — Step-by-Step Protocol
1
Identify and Eliminate Triggers Skin triggers: soap, detergents, synthetic fabrics (wear cotton), pet dander, dust mites (encase mattress, hot-wash bedding weekly). Food triggers (especially children): dairy, egg, wheat, soy, peanut — trial 4–6 week elimination. Stress (activates mast cells — psychodermatology). Sweating (heat, exercise).
2
Skin Barrier Restoration Moisturise within 3 minutes of bathing (wet skin lock-in technique). Emollient 2–3x/day — emollients are the single most important intervention. Preferred: colloidal oatmeal, ceramide-containing creams, shea butter, coconut oil (antimicrobial for S. aureus on eczema skin). Bath temperature: warm not hot (hot strips skin lipids). Bath additives: colloidal oatmeal, Dead Sea salts, evening primrose oil in water.
3
Internal Treatment Evening primrose oil 3–4g/day ★ (GLA reduces inflammatory cytokines). Fish oil EPA 3g/day. Probiotics (L. rhamnosus GG from pregnancy + infant use reduces eczema 50% — prophylactic evidence). Vit D3 4000IU (deficiency strongly linked to eczema severity). Zinc 30mg (reduces S. aureus colonisation). Quercetin 500mg BD (mast cell stabiliser). Wet wrapping for severe flares.
FUNGAL INFECTIONS — Assessment & Protocols
ConditionAppearanceDiagnosisNaturopathic Treatment
Tinea corporis (ringworm)Annular scaly ring, clear centre, advancing edgeSkin scrapings + KOH microscopyTea tree oil 25–50% topical BD × 4 weeks. Neem oil. Garlic extract. If severe: ketoconazole cream (prescriptive).
Tinea pedis (athlete's foot)Interdigital maceration, scaling, itchClinical + KOHKeep feet dry. Tea tree oil soak. Sodium bicarbonate foot powder. Antifungal cream if not resolving.
Onychomycosis (nail fungus)Thickened, discoloured, brittle nails — yellow/brown/whiteNail clippings for culture (slow — 6 weeks)Vicks VapoRub daily (evidence: 32% mycological cure). Tea tree oil. Oral terbinafine (prescriptive — most effective) 12 weeks. Biotin 5mg/day during treatment. Very slow — nails grow 3mm/month.
Pityriasis versicolorHypo or hyperpigmented patches, fine scale, trunkClinical + Wood's lamp (yellow-green fluorescence)Selenium sulphide shampoo applied 15 min BD × 2 weeks then weekly. Tea tree oil. Ketoconazole shampoo. Recurrence common in summer.

cards.title: Vitiligo, Rosacea & Skin Ageing — Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
VITILIGO
Vitiligo — Autoimmune Melanocyte Destruction
Affects 0.5–1% globally. Association: other autoimmune diseases (thyroid, T1DM, pernicious anaemia, alopecia areata). Psychosocial impact is significant — address in consultation.
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Vit D3 ★★★★ Strong — Vit D deficiency associated with vitiligo severity. D3 has immunomodulatory and melanocyte-stimulating effects4000–10,000 IU/day + topical calcipotriol on patches + Vit K2 100mcg
Alpha lipoic acid★★★ Good600mg/day — antioxidant, reduces oxidative stress on melanocytes
Folic acid + Vit B12★★★ Good — repigmentation shown in 52–64% with sun exposure combinationFolate 5mg + B12 1000mcg daily + controlled sun exposure 10–15 min/day on patches
Polypodium leucotomos★★★ Good — fern extract photoprotectant, antioxidant480mg/day. Used as oral sunscreen and vitiligo adjunct in phototherapy.
PUVA/NB-UVB (medical)★★★★ StrongRefer dermatology. Most effective when combined with nutritional support.
ROSACEA
TriggerManagement
Sun exposure ★SPF 30+ mineral sunscreen (zinc oxide/titanium — less irritating). Broad-brimmed hat.
Hot drinks, alcohol, spicy foodIdentify personal triggers with diary. Switch to cold/tepid drinks.
H. pylori (associated)Test and treat H. pylori — often significantly improves rosacea
Demodex mitesTea tree oil face wash 5% (kills Demodex). Ivermectin cream (prescriptive — most effective).
SupplementsZinc 30mg (anti-inflammatory, reduces papules). Omega-3 3g (anti-inflammatory). Silymarin topical/oral. Azelaic acid 15–20% gel (prescriptive — reduces erythema and papules).
SKIN AGEING — NATUROPATHIC APPROACH
🟡 Evidence-Based Anti-Ageing Interventions
Internal: Collagen peptides 10g/day (reduces wrinkle depth 15% at 12 weeks — RCT). Vit C 1g/day (collagen synthesis cofactor, photoprotective). Astaxanthin 4mg/day (carotenoid antioxidant — reduces UV damage, improves elasticity). Omega-3 3g (membrane fluidity, reduces inflammatory skin ageing). Avoid: smoking (accelerates skin ageing 10-fold via free radicals and vasoconstriction), excess UV, alcohol dehydration, high-sugar diet (AGEs — advanced glycation end products cross-link collagen). External: SPF 30+ daily ★ (single most evidence-based anti-ageing intervention), topical Vit C serum 15–20%, retinoids (prescriptive — most evidence), niacinamide 5% (pore size, pigmentation, barrier).

cards.title: Eczema — Comprehensive Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Atopic Eczema — The Atopic Triad
Eczema + asthma + allergic rhinitis. Driven by: impaired skin barrier (filaggrin gene mutation in 50%), immune dysregulation (Th2 skewed), gut dysbiosis, and environmental triggers. Management targets all four drivers simultaneously.
Trigger CategoryCommon TriggersIdentification
FoodDairy, egg, wheat, soy, peanut, tree nuts (especially in infants)Elimination and rechallenge diary. IgE RAST for immediate reactions. Note: IgG testing not validated.
EnvironmentalDust mites, pet dander, mould, pollen, cold and dry airSkin prick testing, allergen-specific IgE panel. Allergy specialist referral.
IrritantsSoap, detergents, fabric softener, fragrances, chlorine in pools, synthetic fabricsClinical history — exposure history and symptom correlation
StressEmotional stress triggers flares via cortisol-mediated immune dysregulationStress diary alongside symptom diary
1
Skin Barrier Repair (Most Important) Moisturise within 3 minutes of bathing (seal in moisture). Use fragrance-free, hypoallergenic emollient (coconut oil, Cetraben, Diprobase, Aveeno). Apply generously — 500g/week for moderate eczema. Avoid soap — use soap substitutes only. Lukewarm water only (hot worsens barrier function). Wet wrapping: apply emollient generously, wrap in damp bandage, cover with dry bandage — reduces itch and inflammation rapidly.
2
Dietary Interventions Dairy elimination trial 4-6 weeks (most common food trigger — especially in infants). Increase: omega-3 rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed), fermented foods (kefir, yoghurt, kimchi), prebiotic fibre (onion, garlic, oats). Reduce: sugar, processed foods, vegetable oils (omega-6 pro-inflammatory). Evening primrose oil 3g/day or borage oil (GLA — reduces inflammatory prostaglandins).
3
Supplements Probiotics (L.rhamnosus GG + B.longum — specific evidence for eczema severity reduction). Vit D3 4000 IU (deficiency strongly associated with eczema severity). Zinc 15mg (skin healing, immune modulation). Fish oil EPA 3g/day. Quercetin 500mg BD (antihistamine, mast cell stabiliser). Vit B12 topical cream — reduces inflammation in some patients.

cards.title: Fungal Skin Infections & Vitiligo
cards._legacyHtml:
FUNGAL SKIN INFECTIONS
ConditionCausative OrganismClinical FeaturesTreatment
Tinea corporis (ringworm)Trichophyton, MicrosporumAnnular scaly plaque, central clearing, advancing erythematous edgeTopical clotrimazole or terbinafine cream BD x2-4 weeks. Extend 1 week beyond clinical resolution. Tea tree oil 25-50% solution — comparable to clotrimazole in RCT.
Tinea pedis (athlete's foot)Trichophyton rubrum (most common)Macerated, scaling, fissured interdigital spaces. Vesicular or moccasin types.Terbinafine cream BD x1-2 weeks. Keep feet dry — moisture-wicking socks, air dry. Tea tree oil foot soak. Probiotic socks (experimental but promising).
Tinea unguium (onychomycosis)T.rubrum, T.mentagrophytesThickened, discoloured (yellow-brown), crumbling nail — starts distallyLong-term — oral terbinafine 250mg OD x6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails) — prescriptive. Naturopathic: Vicks VapoRub application OD (thymol — antifungal — small case series evidence). Tea tree oil 100% applied daily. Laser therapy (limited evidence). Patience required — full resolution takes 6-12 months.
Pityriasis versicolorMalassezia furfur (skin commensal)Hypo or hyperpigmented macules (trunk, upper arms), fine scale, slightly pruriticKetoconazole 2% shampoo leave-on 5-10 min daily x2 weeks. Selenium sulphide shampoo. Recurrence common — monthly maintenance application in summer. Sunlight exposure often reveals depigmented patches (hypopigmented form).
VITILIGO — Naturopathic Approach
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Ginkgo bilobaGood — two RCTs show halting of spread and some repigmentation40mg TDS (standardised extract)
Vitamin D3 topical + oralGood — calcipotriol topical + oral Vit D3 4000 IUTopical calcipotriol BD + oral 4000 IU/day
Polypodium leucotomosGood — oral fern extract for photoprotection and repigmentation240mg BD during phototherapy
Narrowband UVB phototherapyStrong — gold standard with 70% repigmentation in respondersRefer dermatology — 2-3 sessions/week. Combine with topical tacrolimus or steroids.
Pseudocatalase cream + UVBModerate — targets ROS accumulation in vitiligo patchesTopical + supervised UVB

cards.title: Rosacea & Skin Ageing — Naturopathic Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
ROSACEA — Subtype-Specific Management
SubtypeFeaturesNaturopathic Protocol
Subtype 1 — ErythematotelangiectaticFlushing, persistent redness, visible blood vessels, sensitivityTrigger avoidance (sun, heat, spicy food, alcohol, exercise, stress, hot drinks). Azelaic acid 15% gel BD. SPF50+ daily. Green-tinted primer (optical camouflage). Brimonidine gel (prescriptive for acute flushing).
Subtype 2 — PapulopustularAcne-like papules and pustules (no comedones — distinguishes from acne)Metronidazole 0.75% gel BD (prescriptive). Niacinamide 4% topical. Ivermectin 1% cream (prescriptive — Demodex treatment). Low-glycaemic diet. Probiotics (gut-skin axis).
Subtype 3 — PhymatousSkin thickening, enlarged pores, rhinophyma (bulbous nose) — predominantly menEarly intervention essential — advanced rhinophyma requires surgical laser. Isotretinoin (prescriptive) can slow progression.
Subtype 4 — OcularEye irritation, burning, stinging, blepharitis, chalazionWarm compress on eyes BD. Omega-3 3g/day (reduces inflammatory tear lipids). Refer ophthalmology if vision affected.
T
Rosacea Triggers — Common Sun exposure (most common). Hot drinks and spicy food. Alcohol (especially red wine — histamine). Exercise (flushing from vasodilation). Temperature extremes. Emotional stress. Certain skincare ingredients: alcohol, menthol, camphor, witch hazel, fragrance, AHAs. Protective factors: sun avoidance, mineral SPF, green tea extract topical (anti-inflammatory).
SKIN AGEING — Naturopathic Anti-Ageing Protocol
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
SPF50+ dailyStrong — UV is responsible for 80% of visible skin ageing (photoageing)Broad-spectrum SPF50+ every morning — most important anti-ageing intervention
Vitamin C serum (topical)Strong — antioxidant, collagen synthesis, melanin inhibitionL-ascorbic acid 10-20% serum every morning before SPF
Retinol/Retinoid (topical)Strong — increases collagen, reduces fine linesRetinol 0.1% to 0.5% nightly. Start low (1-2x/week), increase frequency. Avoid in pregnancy.
Collagen peptides (oral)Good — skin hydration, elasticity, wrinkle reductionMarine collagen peptides 10g/day. Vit C 500mg alongside (cofactor for collagen synthesis).
Dietary antioxidantsGood — carotenoids (skin colour and photoprotection), polyphenolsLycopene (tomatoes), beta-carotene (carrots), anthocyanins (berries), astaxanthin 4mg/day (strongest skin antioxidant).

cards.title: Skin Conditions in Skin of Colour
cards._legacyHtml:
Skin of Colour — Critical Knowledge for Indian Practitioners
Fitzpatrick skin types IV–VI (most common in India) present differently from fair skin — many conditions are underdiagnosed or mismanaged because dermatology training materials predominantly show Fitzpatrick types I–III. Indian BNYS practitioners must master skin-of-colour presentations.
ConditionPresentation in Dark Skin (vs fair skin)Naturopathic Approach
MelasmaSymmetrical hyperpigmented patches — face (cheeks, forehead, upper lip). Very common in Indian women (pregnancy, OCP, sun exposure). Can be mistaken for post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or drug-induced.Sun protection ★★★ (SPF30+ broad-spectrum — most important). Niacinamide 4–5% topical ★★★ (inhibits melanosome transfer). Kojic acid 2% ★★. Azelaic acid 15–20% ★★. Vitamin C serum 10–20% ★★ (L-ascorbic acid). Oral tranexamic acid 250mg BD ★★★ (systemic anti-melanogenic — prescription). Avoid: OCP if melasma worsens. Chemical peels cautiously in dark skin (hyperpigmentation risk).
Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation (PIH)Macules/patches at site of prior inflammation — acne, eczema, insect bites. Dark-skinned individuals produce MORE melanin in response to inflammation → PIH more pronounced and persistent (months to years).Prevention: treat underlying inflammation early and aggressively (acne, eczema). Sun protection ★★★ (UV darkens PIH). Niacinamide 5% ★★★. Retinol/retinoid topical (gentle — start low). Alpha-arbutin ★★. AHA exfoliation (glycolic/lactic) — stimulates cell turnover to fade macules. Patience: PIH in dark skin may take 6–18 months to fade with optimal management.
Keloid & Hypertrophic ScarsKeloids: scar tissue beyond wound boundary, elevated, may continue growing. 15–20× more common in dark-skinned individuals. Predilection: chest, shoulder, earlobe, upper back.Prevention: silicone gel sheets ★★★ (applied immediately post-wound healing — most evidence). Vitamin E topical (controversial — limited evidence). Pressure therapy (earring keloids — pressure earrings ★★★). Avoid: unnecessary surgery, piercings in keloid-prone sites. Medical: corticosteroid injection (first-line). Laser (PDL) — adjunct. Cryotherapy for small keloids.
Lichen Planus PigmentosusDiffuse brownish-grey facial pigmentation. Asymptomatic. Very common in South Asian and Middle Eastern skin. Chronic and difficult to treat. May affect sun-exposed and non-exposed areas. Often associated with: henna use, mustard oil, fragrance exposure.Remove causative exposures (henna, mustard oil, fragrance). Sun protection. Tacrolimus 0.1% ★★ (calcineurin inhibitor — reduces pigmentation). Niacinamide. Dermatology referral for refractory cases. Patients need realistic expectations — LPP often recurs.
🟡 Melanoma in Dark Skin — Acral Lentiginous Subtype
Melanoma is less common in dark skin — but more dangerous because: (1) Not protected by melanin (UV not primary cause in dark skin — predisposed to acral lentiginous melanoma — ALM). (2) Diagnosed much later (lesions on palms, soles, under nails — often overlooked). (3) 5-year survival much worse due to late diagnosis. Examine: palms, soles, and nail beds (subungual melanoma — dark streak in nail = Hutchinson's sign) in EVERY skin check of dark-skinned patients. Reggae musician Bob Marley died from untreated subungual ALM. Refer any nail pigmentation stripe immediately for dermatology assessment.

cards.title: Urticaria & Angioedema
cards._legacyHtml:
Angioedema — Know When It Is Dangerous
Angioedema involving tongue, lips, or throat = potential airway emergency. Hereditary angioedema (HAE — C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency) does NOT respond to adrenaline or antihistamines — requires specific C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, or ecallantide. Differentiate from allergic angioedema. Any angioedema with voice change or stridor = EMERGENCY — call 108.
TypeFeaturesDurationManagement
Acute allergic urticariaWheals + flare, intensely pruritic, erythematous, migratory (individual lesion <24hrs). Trigger: food, drug, insect sting.<6 weeksIdentify and avoid trigger. Antihistamine H1+H2 combination (cetirizine + famotidine). Quercetin 500mg ★★. Adrenaline auto-injector if anaphylaxis risk.
Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU)Urticaria >6 weeks with no identifiable external trigger. 80% idiopathic (often autoimmune — anti-FcεRI or anti-IgE antibodies).>6 weeksNon-sedating antihistamine up to 4× normal dose (first-line). Omalizumab (anti-IgE — 300mg SC monthly) for refractory cases. Quercetin 500mg BD ★★★. Low-pseudoallergen diet trial ★★.
Physical urticariaTriggered by physical stimuli: dermographism (stroking), cold, heat, pressure, vibration, exercise, sun (solar urticaria).Minutes to hoursTrigger avoidance. Antihistamine prophylactically. Cold urticaria: cyproheptadine. Exercise-induced: antihistamine before exercise.
Hereditary angioedema (HAE)Recurrent episodes of non-pitting oedema — face, extremities, genitalia, gut (severe abdominal pain), larynx. No wheals. Family history (AD inheritance). NO histamine involved → antihistamines ineffective.2–5 days per episodeREFER URGENTLY to immunology. C1-INH concentrate or icatibant for acute attack. Tranexamic acid or danazol for prophylaxis. Adrenaline does NOT work for HAE.
1
Low-Pseudoallergen Diet for CSU Pseudoallergens are natural food chemicals that cause non-IgE-mediated urticaria by direct mast cell activation. Low-pseudoallergen diet avoids: salicylates (fruits, vegetables, spices — high-salicylate: tomato, citrus, berries, capsicum), biogenic amines (histamine from aged/fermented foods, tyramine from cheese, wine, processed meats), benzoates (preservatives), azo dyes (food colouring). Trial: strict low-pseudoallergen diet for 3 weeks → if 50%+ improvement → reintroduce one group at a time. Supplements: quercetin 500mg BD (mast cell stabiliser), Vit C 1g (DAO enzyme cofactor — degrades histamine), DAO enzyme supplement (takes histamine from food before absorption).

cards.title: Nail Disorders — Clinical Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Nails as Systemic Diagnostic Windows
Nail changes are often the first visible sign of systemic disease — and are frequently overlooked. A thorough nail examination should be part of every physical assessment in BNYS practice.
Nail SignAppearanceAssociated ConditionsAction
Clubbing↑ Lovibond angle (>180°). Loss of diamond window (Schamroth's sign). Spongy, boggy nail bed on pressure.Respiratory: bronchiectasis, IPF, lung cancer, cystic fibrosis. Cardiovascular: cyanotic CHD, IE. GI: IBD, cirrhosis. Thyroid: thyroid acropachy (clubbing + periosteal changes in Graves'). Normal in some families (pachydermoperiostosis).Refer for chest XR + echo. Investigate for underlying disease. New onset clubbing in adult = urgent workup.
Koilonychia (spoon nails)Concave, spoon-shaped nails. Can hold a drop of water.Iron deficiency anaemia ★★★ (most common cause). Haemochromatosis. Hypothyroidism. Normal in young children.Check FBC, serum ferritin, TIBC. Treat iron deficiency. Reverses with correction of iron status.
Leukonychia (white nails)White spots (true leukonychia — zinc deficiency). White bands — Muehrcke's (parallel white bands = hypoalbuminaemia <2g/dL). Terry's nails (2/3 white, 1/3 red-pink distal tip = cirrhosis, CCF, T2DM). Lindsay's nails (half-and-half = CKD).White spots: zinc deficiency, minor trauma. Muehrcke's: hypoalbuminaemia (cirrhosis, nephrotic, malnutrition). Terry's: systemic disease. Lindsay's: CKD.Zinc 25mg, protein + calorie status. Check albumin. Renal function if Lindsay's.
OnycholysisSeparation of nail plate from nail bed (distal to proximal). Yellow-white area under nail. Painful if acute.Thyroid disease ★★★ (especially hyperthyroidism — Plummer's nails). Psoriasis. Fungal. Trauma. Photo-onycholysis (tetracyclines + sun).Check TSH. Rule out fungal (KOH prep or culture). Psoriasis: look for plaque elsewhere. Keep nails short and dry.
Nail pittingSmall irregular depressions in nail plate surfacePsoriasis ★★★ (most common — >50% of psoriasis patients). Alopecia areata. Eczema.Look for psoriatic plaques elsewhere. Enquire about joint pain (psoriatic arthritis). Topical treatment per underlying condition.
Beau's linesTransverse grooves across nail plate — reflect temporary arrest of nail growth. Severity reflects duration of insult. Multiple nails = systemic cause.Severe systemic illness, high fever, chemotherapy, myocardial infarction, major surgery. Single nail = local trauma.Identify the timing (nail grows 3mm/month → distance from cuticle dates the event). No specific treatment — documents past illness.

cards.title: Skin Manifestations of Systemic Disease
cards._legacyHtml:
Skin as a Mirror of Systemic Health
The skin is the largest organ and often the first to show systemic disease. Many serious conditions — diabetes, liver disease, thyroid dysfunction, lupus, and internal malignancy — have characteristic cutaneous markers. Recognising these signs enables early diagnosis and appropriate referral.
Skin SignAppearanceConditionAction
Acanthosis NigricansVelvety, darkened, thickened skin in flexural areas (neck, axillae, groin). Skin tags often co-present.Insulin resistance ★★★ (most common cause). PCOS. Obesity. Rarely: gastric carcinoma (paraneoplastic — rapid onset, extensive = malignancy until proven otherwise).Check fasting glucose + insulin, HOMA-IR. If rapid onset in older adult without metabolic risk factors: urgent endoscopy. Insulin resistance management.
Xanthelasma / XanthomaXanthelasma: yellowish plaques around eyelids. Xanthoma: yellowish deposits in tendons (Achilles, extensor tendons). Eruptive xanthoma: yellow papules on buttocks, elbows.Dyslipidaemia (familial hypercholesterolaemia — Achilles xanthoma pathognomonic). Eruptive xanthoma = severe hypertriglyceridaemia (>11 mmol/L) — pancreatitis risk.Fasting lipid panel. Eruptive xanthoma = urgent TG management. Familial hypercholesterolaemia: cardiology referral. Xanthelasma: 50% have normal lipids.
Diabetic DermopathyLight brown, slightly scaly patches on anterior shins (shin spots). 50% of T2DM patients. Painless.Diabetes mellitus (most common cutaneous manifestation of DM). Secondary to microangiopathy + neuropathy.Diagnose diabetes if not already known. Optimise glucose control. No specific treatment for dermopathy itself.
Spider Naevi / Palmar ErythemaSpider naevus: central arteriole + radiating vessels (>5 is pathological — a few are normal in pregnancy and children). Palmar erythema: erythema of thenar and hypothenar eminences. Leukonychia: pale nails from hypoalbuminaemia.Liver disease ★★★ (cirrhosis, hepatitis — ↑ oestrogen from reduced hepatic metabolism). Pregnancy (normal). Hyperthyroidism.Check LFTs + GGT + PT (liver synthetic function). Refer gastroenterology if >5 spider naevi or other signs of chronic liver disease.
Butterfly Rash (malar rash)Erythematous rash over cheeks and nose bridge — spares nasolabial folds (distinguishes from rosacea which affects nasolabial folds).SLE ★★★ (systemic lupus erythematosus). Also: rosacea (affects nasolabial folds), dermatomyositis (heliotrope rash), seborrhoeic dermatitis.ANA + anti-dsDNA + complement levels. Refer rheumatology if ANA positive. Photosensitivity is pathognomonic for SLE malar rash.
Pretibial MyxoedemaNon-pitting, brawny, yellow-orange plaque on anterior shins. Orange peel texture. Rare.Graves' disease ★★★ (hyperthyroidism — autoimmune). Thyroid dysfunction.Check TSH + TRAb (Graves' antibody). Refer endocrinology. Local corticosteroid intralesional or topical under occlusion.

cards.title: Acne — Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
1
Diet Modification ★ Low-glycaemic diet (most important): eliminate white bread, sugar, rice, cereals. Each unit ↑ in GL → 13% ↑ acne risk. Eliminate dairy (IGF-1 and whey protein are acnegenic). Add: low-sugar foods, omega-3 (sardines, flaxseed), colourful antioxidant vegetables.
2
Key Supplements Zinc 30mg/day (reduces 5-alpha reductase, anti-inflammatory, equals tetracycline in mild-moderate acne — RCT evidence). Niacinamide 500mg BD (sebum regulation). Vit A 10,000 IU (3 months, not in pregnancy). Fish oil 2g EPA/day.
3
Topical Treatments Tea tree oil 5% gel = equivalent to benzoyl peroxide 5% (RCT evidence). Niacinamide 4–5% cream (reduces sebum, anti-inflammatory). Aloe vera gel (anti-inflammatory, promotes healing). Manuka honey (antimicrobial, wound healing).
4
Hormonal Acne (PCOS) Spearmint tea 2 cups/day (anti-androgenic — reduces free testosterone). Chaste tree (Vitex) 400mg/day (LH/FSH regulation). Inositol 2–4g/day. Address insulin resistance with low-GI diet + exercise.
🟡 Gut-Skin Axis
54% of acne patients have significant gut dysbiosis. Treat the gut: probiotics (Lactobacillus acidophilus + Bifidobacterium), eliminate constipation (toxin reabsorption), identify food intolerances. If hormonal acne + GI symptoms + hirsutism → screen for PCOS (ultrasound, testosterone, LH:FSH ratio, fasting insulin).

cards.title: Psoriasis — Naturopathic Management
cards._legacyHtml:
InterventionEvidenceDose/ProtocolMechanism
Fish oil (EPA+DHA)★★★ Good3–4g EPA/dayCompetes with arachidonic acid, reduces LTB4 (key psoriasis mediator)
Vitamin D3★★★★ Strong4000–6000 IU/day + topical calcipotriolKeratinocyte differentiation, immune modulation
Curcumin★★★ Good1000mg with piperine BDNF-κB inhibition, TNF-α reduction
Mahonia aquifolium (Oregon grape) topical★★★ Good10% cream BDInhibits keratinocyte proliferation
Gluten-free diet★★ ModerateStrict GFD if anti-gliadin Ab+Reduces autoimmune trigger in coeliac-associated psoriasis
Dead Sea salt baths★★★ Good25% salt solution, 30 min, 3x/weekMineral absorption (Mg, Br), UVB exposure
Stress management★★★ GoodMBSR, yoga, meditationHPA axis normalisation, neurogenic inflammation ↓
🟡 Psoriasis + Metabolic Syndrome
Psoriasis is NOT just a skin disease — it is a systemic inflammatory condition. 30% have psoriatic arthritis. 40–60% have metabolic syndrome. Screen every psoriasis patient for: T2DM (HbA1c), dyslipidaemia (lipid panel), hypertension, depression, and CVD risk. Treating metabolic syndrome IMPROVES psoriasis severity independently of skin-directed treatment.

cards.title: Eczema — Comprehensive Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
Atopic Eczema — The Atopic Triad
Eczema + asthma + allergic rhinitis. Driven by: impaired skin barrier (filaggrin gene mutation in 50%), immune dysregulation (Th2 skewed), gut dysbiosis, and environmental triggers. Management targets all four drivers simultaneously.
Trigger CategoryCommon TriggersIdentification
FoodDairy, egg, wheat, soy, peanut, tree nuts (especially in infants)Elimination and rechallenge diary. IgE RAST for immediate reactions. Note: IgG testing not validated.
EnvironmentalDust mites, pet dander, mould, pollen, cold and dry airSkin prick testing, allergen-specific IgE panel. Allergy specialist referral.
IrritantsSoap, detergents, fabric softener, fragrances, chlorine in pools, synthetic fabricsClinical history — exposure history and symptom correlation
StressEmotional stress triggers flares via cortisol-mediated immune dysregulationStress diary alongside symptom diary
1
Skin Barrier Repair (Most Important) Moisturise within 3 minutes of bathing (seal in moisture). Use fragrance-free, hypoallergenic emollient (coconut oil, Cetraben, Diprobase, Aveeno). Apply generously — 500g/week for moderate eczema. Avoid soap — use soap substitutes only. Lukewarm water only (hot worsens barrier function). Wet wrapping: apply emollient generously, wrap in damp bandage, cover with dry bandage — reduces itch and inflammation rapidly.
2
Dietary Interventions Dairy elimination trial 4-6 weeks (most common food trigger — especially in infants). Increase: omega-3 rich foods (oily fish, flaxseed), fermented foods (kefir, yoghurt, kimchi), prebiotic fibre (onion, garlic, oats). Reduce: sugar, processed foods, vegetable oils (omega-6 pro-inflammatory). Evening primrose oil 3g/day or borage oil (GLA — reduces inflammatory prostaglandins).
3
Supplements Probiotics (L.rhamnosus GG + B.longum — specific evidence for eczema severity reduction). Vit D3 4000 IU (deficiency strongly associated with eczema severity). Zinc 15mg (skin healing, immune modulation). Fish oil EPA 3g/day. Quercetin 500mg BD (antihistamine, mast cell stabiliser). Vit B12 topical cream — reduces inflammation in some patients.

cards.title: Hair Loss — Assessment & Naturopathic Protocol
cards._legacyHtml:
TypePatternRoot CausesNaturopathic Protocol
Androgenic alopecia (M)Hamilton-Norwood recession, vertexDHT sensitivity, insulin resistanceSaw palmetto 320mg/day, zinc 30mg, biotin 5mg, pumpkin seed oil 400mg
Androgenic alopecia (F)Ludwig — central thinningPCOS, iron deficiency, menopauseCheck ferritin (target >70), TSH, androgens; spearmint tea BD, iron if deficient
Telogen effluviumDiffuse shedding 2–4 months after triggerIllness, surgery, childbirth, rapid weight loss, stressIdentify and remove trigger, ferritin >70, zinc, biotin, protein 1.2g/kg — resolves 6–9 months
Alopecia areataPatchy loss, exclamation mark hairsAutoimmune trigger, stressVit D, zinc, probiotics, stress management — refer dermatology for intralesional steroids
Seborrhoeic dermatitisFlaky oily scalp, MalasseziaDysbiosis, low zinc/B6, stress, sugarKetoconazole 2% shampoo EOD × 4 weeks, then zinc pyrithione maintenance, probiotics, B6
🟡 Ferritin Target for Hair
Lab normal for ferritin (>12 µg/L) is insufficient for hair growth. Target ferritin >70 µg/L for hair health. At ferritin <40: shedding increases significantly. Supplement: iron bisglycinate 25–50mg with Vit C 250mg, recheck in 3 months. Full hair loss workup: ferritin, B12, folate, zinc, TSH, free T4, testosterone, DHEA-S, SHBG. In women — always exclude PCOS and thyroid disease before treating hair loss symptomatically.

cards.title: Eczema & Fungal Infections — Naturopathic Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
ATOPIC ECZEMA — Step-by-Step Protocol
1
Identify and Eliminate Triggers Skin triggers: soap, detergents, synthetic fabrics (wear cotton), pet dander, dust mites (encase mattress, hot-wash bedding weekly). Food triggers (especially children): dairy, egg, wheat, soy, peanut — trial 4–6 week elimination. Stress (activates mast cells — psychodermatology). Sweating (heat, exercise).
2
Skin Barrier Restoration Moisturise within 3 minutes of bathing (wet skin lock-in technique). Emollient 2–3x/day — emollients are the single most important intervention. Preferred: colloidal oatmeal, ceramide-containing creams, shea butter, coconut oil (antimicrobial for S. aureus on eczema skin). Bath temperature: warm not hot (hot strips skin lipids). Bath additives: colloidal oatmeal, Dead Sea salts, evening primrose oil in water.
3
Internal Treatment Evening primrose oil 3–4g/day ★ (GLA reduces inflammatory cytokines). Fish oil EPA 3g/day. Probiotics (L. rhamnosus GG from pregnancy + infant use reduces eczema 50% — prophylactic evidence). Vit D3 4000IU (deficiency strongly linked to eczema severity). Zinc 30mg (reduces S. aureus colonisation). Quercetin 500mg BD (mast cell stabiliser). Wet wrapping for severe flares.
FUNGAL INFECTIONS — Assessment & Protocols
ConditionAppearanceDiagnosisNaturopathic Treatment
Tinea corporis (ringworm)Annular scaly ring, clear centre, advancing edgeSkin scrapings + KOH microscopyTea tree oil 25–50% topical BD × 4 weeks. Neem oil. Garlic extract. If severe: ketoconazole cream (prescriptive).
Tinea pedis (athlete's foot)Interdigital maceration, scaling, itchClinical + KOHKeep feet dry. Tea tree oil soak. Sodium bicarbonate foot powder. Antifungal cream if not resolving.
Onychomycosis (nail fungus)Thickened, discoloured, brittle nails — yellow/brown/whiteNail clippings for culture (slow — 6 weeks)Vicks VapoRub daily (evidence: 32% mycological cure). Tea tree oil. Oral terbinafine (prescriptive — most effective) 12 weeks. Biotin 5mg/day during treatment. Very slow — nails grow 3mm/month.
Pityriasis versicolorHypo or hyperpigmented patches, fine scale, trunkClinical + Wood's lamp (yellow-green fluorescence)Selenium sulphide shampoo applied 15 min BD × 2 weeks then weekly. Tea tree oil. Ketoconazole shampoo. Recurrence common in summer.

cards.title: Fungal Skin Infections & Vitiligo
cards._legacyHtml:
FUNGAL SKIN INFECTIONS
ConditionCausative OrganismClinical FeaturesTreatment
Tinea corporis (ringworm)Trichophyton, MicrosporumAnnular scaly plaque, central clearing, advancing erythematous edgeTopical clotrimazole or terbinafine cream BD x2-4 weeks. Extend 1 week beyond clinical resolution. Tea tree oil 25-50% solution — comparable to clotrimazole in RCT.
Tinea pedis (athlete's foot)Trichophyton rubrum (most common)Macerated, scaling, fissured interdigital spaces. Vesicular or moccasin types.Terbinafine cream BD x1-2 weeks. Keep feet dry — moisture-wicking socks, air dry. Tea tree oil foot soak. Probiotic socks (experimental but promising).
Tinea unguium (onychomycosis)T.rubrum, T.mentagrophytesThickened, discoloured (yellow-brown), crumbling nail — starts distallyLong-term — oral terbinafine 250mg OD x6 weeks (fingernails) or 12 weeks (toenails) — prescriptive. Naturopathic: Vicks VapoRub application OD (thymol — antifungal — small case series evidence). Tea tree oil 100% applied daily. Laser therapy (limited evidence). Patience required — full resolution takes 6-12 months.
Pityriasis versicolorMalassezia furfur (skin commensal)Hypo or hyperpigmented macules (trunk, upper arms), fine scale, slightly pruriticKetoconazole 2% shampoo leave-on 5-10 min daily x2 weeks. Selenium sulphide shampoo. Recurrence common — monthly maintenance application in summer. Sunlight exposure often reveals depigmented patches (hypopigmented form).
VITILIGO — Naturopathic Approach
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Ginkgo bilobaGood — two RCTs show halting of spread and some repigmentation40mg TDS (standardised extract)
Vitamin D3 topical + oralGood — calcipotriol topical + oral Vit D3 4000 IUTopical calcipotriol BD + oral 4000 IU/day
Polypodium leucotomosGood — oral fern extract for photoprotection and repigmentation240mg BD during phototherapy
Narrowband UVB phototherapyStrong — gold standard with 70% repigmentation in respondersRefer dermatology — 2-3 sessions/week. Combine with topical tacrolimus or steroids.
Pseudocatalase cream + UVBModerate — targets ROS accumulation in vitiligo patchesTopical + supervised UVB

cards.title: Vitiligo, Rosacea & Skin Ageing — Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
VITILIGO
Vitiligo — Autoimmune Melanocyte Destruction
Affects 0.5–1% globally. Association: other autoimmune diseases (thyroid, T1DM, pernicious anaemia, alopecia areata). Psychosocial impact is significant — address in consultation.
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
Vit D3 ★★★★ Strong — Vit D deficiency associated with vitiligo severity. D3 has immunomodulatory and melanocyte-stimulating effects4000–10,000 IU/day + topical calcipotriol on patches + Vit K2 100mcg
Alpha lipoic acid★★★ Good600mg/day — antioxidant, reduces oxidative stress on melanocytes
Folic acid + Vit B12★★★ Good — repigmentation shown in 52–64% with sun exposure combinationFolate 5mg + B12 1000mcg daily + controlled sun exposure 10–15 min/day on patches
Polypodium leucotomos★★★ Good — fern extract photoprotectant, antioxidant480mg/day. Used as oral sunscreen and vitiligo adjunct in phototherapy.
PUVA/NB-UVB (medical)★★★★ StrongRefer dermatology. Most effective when combined with nutritional support.
ROSACEA
TriggerManagement
Sun exposure ★SPF 30+ mineral sunscreen (zinc oxide/titanium — less irritating). Broad-brimmed hat.
Hot drinks, alcohol, spicy foodIdentify personal triggers with diary. Switch to cold/tepid drinks.
H. pylori (associated)Test and treat H. pylori — often significantly improves rosacea
Demodex mitesTea tree oil face wash 5% (kills Demodex). Ivermectin cream (prescriptive — most effective).
SupplementsZinc 30mg (anti-inflammatory, reduces papules). Omega-3 3g (anti-inflammatory). Silymarin topical/oral. Azelaic acid 15–20% gel (prescriptive — reduces erythema and papules).
SKIN AGEING — NATUROPATHIC APPROACH
🟡 Evidence-Based Anti-Ageing Interventions
Internal: Collagen peptides 10g/day (reduces wrinkle depth 15% at 12 weeks — RCT). Vit C 1g/day (collagen synthesis cofactor, photoprotective). Astaxanthin 4mg/day (carotenoid antioxidant — reduces UV damage, improves elasticity). Omega-3 3g (membrane fluidity, reduces inflammatory skin ageing). Avoid: smoking (accelerates skin ageing 10-fold via free radicals and vasoconstriction), excess UV, alcohol dehydration, high-sugar diet (AGEs — advanced glycation end products cross-link collagen). External: SPF 30+ daily ★ (single most evidence-based anti-ageing intervention), topical Vit C serum 15–20%, retinoids (prescriptive — most evidence), niacinamide 5% (pore size, pigmentation, barrier).

cards.title: Rosacea & Skin Ageing — Naturopathic Protocols
cards._legacyHtml:
ROSACEA — Subtype-Specific Management
SubtypeFeaturesNaturopathic Protocol
Subtype 1 — ErythematotelangiectaticFlushing, persistent redness, visible blood vessels, sensitivityTrigger avoidance (sun, heat, spicy food, alcohol, exercise, stress, hot drinks). Azelaic acid 15% gel BD. SPF50+ daily. Green-tinted primer (optical camouflage). Brimonidine gel (prescriptive for acute flushing).
Subtype 2 — PapulopustularAcne-like papules and pustules (no comedones — distinguishes from acne)Metronidazole 0.75% gel BD (prescriptive). Niacinamide 4% topical. Ivermectin 1% cream (prescriptive — Demodex treatment). Low-glycaemic diet. Probiotics (gut-skin axis).
Subtype 3 — PhymatousSkin thickening, enlarged pores, rhinophyma (bulbous nose) — predominantly menEarly intervention essential — advanced rhinophyma requires surgical laser. Isotretinoin (prescriptive) can slow progression.
Subtype 4 — OcularEye irritation, burning, stinging, blepharitis, chalazionWarm compress on eyes BD. Omega-3 3g/day (reduces inflammatory tear lipids). Refer ophthalmology if vision affected.
T
Rosacea Triggers — Common Sun exposure (most common). Hot drinks and spicy food. Alcohol (especially red wine — histamine). Exercise (flushing from vasodilation). Temperature extremes. Emotional stress. Certain skincare ingredients: alcohol, menthol, camphor, witch hazel, fragrance, AHAs. Protective factors: sun avoidance, mineral SPF, green tea extract topical (anti-inflammatory).
SKIN AGEING — Naturopathic Anti-Ageing Protocol
InterventionEvidenceProtocol
SPF50+ dailyStrong — UV is responsible for 80% of visible skin ageing (photoageing)Broad-spectrum SPF50+ every morning — most important anti-ageing intervention
Vitamin C serum (topical)Strong — antioxidant, collagen synthesis, melanin inhibitionL-ascorbic acid 10-20% serum every morning before SPF
Retinol/Retinoid (topical)Strong — increases collagen, reduces fine linesRetinol 0.1% to 0.5% nightly. Start low (1-2x/week), increase frequency. Avoid in pregnancy.
Collagen peptides (oral)Good — skin hydration, elasticity, wrinkle reductionMarine collagen peptides 10g/day. Vit C 500mg alongside (cofactor for collagen synthesis).
Dietary antioxidantsGood — carotenoids (skin colour and photoprotection), polyphenolsLycopene (tomatoes), beta-carotene (carrots), anthocyanins (berries), astaxanthin 4mg/day (strongest skin antioxidant).

cards.title: Skin Conditions in Skin of Colour
cards._legacyHtml:
Skin of Colour — Critical Knowledge for Indian Practitioners
Fitzpatrick skin types IV–VI (most common in India) present differently from fair skin — many conditions are underdiagnosed or mismanaged because dermatology training materials predominantly show Fitzpatrick types I–III. Indian BNYS practitioners must master skin-of-colour presentations.
ConditionPresentation in Dark Skin (vs fair skin)Naturopathic Approach
MelasmaSymmetrical hyperpigmented patches — face (cheeks, forehead, upper lip). Very common in Indian women (pregnancy, OCP, sun exposure). Can be mistaken for post-inflammatory hyperpigmentation or drug-induced.Sun protection ★★★ (SPF30+ broad-spectrum — most important). Niacinamide 4–5% topical ★★★ (inhibits melanosome transfer). Kojic acid 2% ★★. Azelaic acid 15–20% ★★. Vitamin C serum 10–20% ★★ (L-ascorbic acid). Oral tranexamic acid 250mg BD ★★★ (systemic anti-melanogenic — prescription). Avoid: OCP if melasma worsens. Chemical peels cautiously in dark skin (hyperpigmentation risk).
Post-Inflammatory Hyperpigmentation (PIH)Macules/patches at site of prior inflammation — acne, eczema, insect bites. Dark-skinned individuals produce MORE melanin in response to inflammation → PIH more pronounced and persistent (months to years).Prevention: treat underlying inflammation early and aggressively (acne, eczema). Sun protection ★★★ (UV darkens PIH). Niacinamide 5% ★★★. Retinol/retinoid topical (gentle — start low). Alpha-arbutin ★★. AHA exfoliation (glycolic/lactic) — stimulates cell turnover to fade macules. Patience: PIH in dark skin may take 6–18 months to fade with optimal management.
Keloid & Hypertrophic ScarsKeloids: scar tissue beyond wound boundary, elevated, may continue growing. 15–20× more common in dark-skinned individuals. Predilection: chest, shoulder, earlobe, upper back.Prevention: silicone gel sheets ★★★ (applied immediately post-wound healing — most evidence). Vitamin E topical (controversial — limited evidence). Pressure therapy (earring keloids — pressure earrings ★★★). Avoid: unnecessary surgery, piercings in keloid-prone sites. Medical: corticosteroid injection (first-line). Laser (PDL) — adjunct. Cryotherapy for small keloids.
Lichen Planus PigmentosusDiffuse brownish-grey facial pigmentation. Asymptomatic. Very common in South Asian and Middle Eastern skin. Chronic and difficult to treat. May affect sun-exposed and non-exposed areas. Often associated with: henna use, mustard oil, fragrance exposure.Remove causative exposures (henna, mustard oil, fragrance). Sun protection. Tacrolimus 0.1% ★★ (calcineurin inhibitor — reduces pigmentation). Niacinamide. Dermatology referral for refractory cases. Patients need realistic expectations — LPP often recurs.
🟡 Melanoma in Dark Skin — Acral Lentiginous Subtype
Melanoma is less common in dark skin — but more dangerous because: (1) Not protected by melanin (UV not primary cause in dark skin — predisposed to acral lentiginous melanoma — ALM). (2) Diagnosed much later (lesions on palms, soles, under nails — often overlooked). (3) 5-year survival much worse due to late diagnosis. Examine: palms, soles, and nail beds (subungual melanoma — dark streak in nail = Hutchinson's sign) in EVERY skin check of dark-skinned patients. Reggae musician Bob Marley died from untreated subungual ALM. Refer any nail pigmentation stripe immediately for dermatology assessment.

cards.title: Dermatology — Common Skin Conditions Overview
cards._legacyHtml:
ConditionKey FeaturesTriggersNaturopathic Approach
Atopic eczemaPruritic, flexural, dry skin, IgE-mediatedAllergens, stress, heat, soap, woolElimination diet, probiotics, Vit D, evening primrose oil, topical coconut oil
PsoriasisSilvery plaques, Auspitz sign, nail pittingStress, infection (strep), alcohol, NSAIDsFish oil 3–4g/day, Vit D3+K2, elimination diet, stress management, aloe vera topical
Acne vulgarisComedones, papules, pustules, nodulesHormones, dairy, high-GI foods, stressLow-GI diet, eliminate dairy, zinc 30mg, niacinamide topical, tea tree oil 5%
RosaceaFacial erythema, telangiectasia, papulopustulesSun, alcohol, spicy food, heat, stressAzelaic acid, green tea extract, avoid triggers, Vit B3, treat SIBO (associated)
UrticariaWheals, intense itch, angioedemaAllergens, aspirin/NSAIDs, infection, stressQuercetin 500mg BD, Vit C, identify and remove trigger, antihistamine herbs
Seborrhoeic dermatitisDandruff, greasy scales, scalp/nasolabialMalassezia yeast, stress, coldZinc pyrithione shampoo, selenium sulphide, probiotics, Vit B6, reduce sugar

cards.title: Urticaria & Angioedema
cards._legacyHtml:
Angioedema — Know When It Is Dangerous
Angioedema involving tongue, lips, or throat = potential airway emergency. Hereditary angioedema (HAE — C1 esterase inhibitor deficiency) does NOT respond to adrenaline or antihistamines — requires specific C1-INH concentrate, icatibant, or ecallantide. Differentiate from allergic angioedema. Any angioedema with voice change or stridor = EMERGENCY — call 108.
TypeFeaturesDurationManagement
Acute allergic urticariaWheals + flare, intensely pruritic, erythematous, migratory (individual lesion <24hrs). Trigger: food, drug, insect sting.<6 weeksIdentify and avoid trigger. Antihistamine H1+H2 combination (cetirizine + famotidine). Quercetin 500mg ★★. Adrenaline auto-injector if anaphylaxis risk.
Chronic spontaneous urticaria (CSU)Urticaria >6 weeks with no identifiable external trigger. 80% idiopathic (often autoimmune — anti-FcεRI or anti-IgE antibodies).>6 weeksNon-sedating antihistamine up to 4× normal dose (first-line). Omalizumab (anti-IgE — 300mg SC monthly) for refractory cases. Quercetin 500mg BD ★★★. Low-pseudoallergen diet trial ★★.
Physical urticariaTriggered by physical stimuli: dermographism (stroking), cold, heat, pressure, vibration, exercise, sun (solar urticaria).Minutes to hoursTrigger avoidance. Antihistamine prophylactically. Cold urticaria: cyproheptadine. Exercise-induced: antihistamine before exercise.
Hereditary angioedema (HAE)Recurrent episodes of non-pitting oedema — face, extremities, genitalia, gut (severe abdominal pain), larynx. No wheals. Family history (AD inheritance). NO histamine involved → antihistamines ineffective.2–5 days per episodeREFER URGENTLY to immunology. C1-INH concentrate or icatibant for acute attack. Tranexamic acid or danazol for prophylaxis. Adrenaline does NOT work for HAE.
1
Low-Pseudoallergen Diet for CSU Pseudoallergens are natural food chemicals that cause non-IgE-mediated urticaria by direct mast cell activation. Low-pseudoallergen diet avoids: salicylates (fruits, vegetables, spices — high-salicylate: tomato, citrus, berries, capsicum), biogenic amines (histamine from aged/fermented foods, tyramine from cheese, wine, processed meats), benzoates (preservatives), azo dyes (food colouring). Trial: strict low-pseudoallergen diet for 3 weeks → if 50%+ improvement → reintroduce one group at a time. Supplements: quercetin 500mg BD (mast cell stabiliser), Vit C 1g (DAO enzyme cofactor — degrades histamine), DAO enzyme supplement (takes histamine from food before absorption).

cards.title: Nail Disorders — Clinical Guide
cards._legacyHtml:
Nails as Systemic Diagnostic Windows
Nail changes are often the first visible sign of systemic disease — and are frequently overlooked. A thorough nail examination should be part of every physical assessment in BNYS practice.
Nail SignAppearanceAssociated ConditionsAction
Clubbing↑ Lovibond angle (>180°). Loss of diamond window (Schamroth's sign). Spongy, boggy nail bed on pressure.Respiratory: bronchiectasis, IPF, lung cancer, cystic fibrosis. Cardiovascular: cyanotic CHD, IE. GI: IBD, cirrhosis. Thyroid: thyroid acropachy (clubbing + periosteal changes in Graves'). Normal in some families (pachydermoperiostosis).Refer for chest XR + echo. Investigate for underlying disease. New onset clubbing in adult = urgent workup.
Koilonychia (spoon nails)Concave, spoon-shaped nails. Can hold a drop of water.Iron deficiency anaemia ★★★ (most common cause). Haemochromatosis. Hypothyroidism. Normal in young children.Check FBC, serum ferritin, TIBC. Treat iron deficiency. Reverses with correction of iron status.
Leukonychia (white nails)White spots (true leukonychia — zinc deficiency). White bands — Muehrcke's (parallel white bands = hypoalbuminaemia <2g/dL). Terry's nails (2/3 white, 1/3 red-pink distal tip = cirrhosis, CCF, T2DM). Lindsay's nails (half-and-half = CKD).White spots: zinc deficiency, minor trauma. Muehrcke's: hypoalbuminaemia (cirrhosis, nephrotic, malnutrition). Terry's: systemic disease. Lindsay's: CKD.Zinc 25mg, protein + calorie status. Check albumin. Renal function if Lindsay's.
OnycholysisSeparation of nail plate from nail bed (distal to proximal). Yellow-white area under nail. Painful if acute.Thyroid disease ★★★ (especially hyperthyroidism — Plummer's nails). Psoriasis. Fungal. Trauma. Photo-onycholysis (tetracyclines + sun).Check TSH. Rule out fungal (KOH prep or culture). Psoriasis: look for plaque elsewhere. Keep nails short and dry.
Nail pittingSmall irregular depressions in nail plate surfacePsoriasis ★★★ (most common — >50% of psoriasis patients). Alopecia areata. Eczema.Look for psoriatic plaques elsewhere. Enquire about joint pain (psoriatic arthritis). Topical treatment per underlying condition.
Beau's linesTransverse grooves across nail plate — reflect temporary arrest of nail growth. Severity reflects duration of insult. Multiple nails = systemic cause.Severe systemic illness, high fever, chemotherapy, myocardial infarction, major surgery. Single nail = local trauma.Identify the timing (nail grows 3mm/month → distance from cuticle dates the event). No specific treatment — documents past illness.

cards.title: Skin Manifestations of Systemic Disease
cards._legacyHtml:
Skin as a Mirror of Systemic Health
The skin is the largest organ and often the first to show systemic disease. Many serious conditions — diabetes, liver disease, thyroid dysfunction, lupus, and internal malignancy — have characteristic cutaneous markers. Recognising these signs enables early diagnosis and appropriate referral.
Skin SignAppearanceConditionAction
Acanthosis NigricansVelvety, darkened, thickened skin in flexural areas (neck, axillae, groin). Skin tags often co-present.Insulin resistance ★★★ (most common cause). PCOS. Obesity. Rarely: gastric carcinoma (paraneoplastic — rapid onset, extensive = malignancy until proven otherwise).Check fasting glucose + insulin, HOMA-IR. If rapid onset in older adult without metabolic risk factors: urgent endoscopy. Insulin resistance management.
Xanthelasma / XanthomaXanthelasma: yellowish plaques around eyelids. Xanthoma: yellowish deposits in tendons (Achilles, extensor tendons). Eruptive xanthoma: yellow papules on buttocks, elbows.Dyslipidaemia (familial hypercholesterolaemia — Achilles xanthoma pathognomonic). Eruptive xanthoma = severe hypertriglyceridaemia (>11 mmol/L) — pancreatitis risk.Fasting lipid panel. Eruptive xanthoma = urgent TG management. Familial hypercholesterolaemia: cardiology referral. Xanthelasma: 50% have normal lipids.
Diabetic DermopathyLight brown, slightly scaly patches on anterior shins (shin spots). 50% of T2DM patients. Painless.Diabetes mellitus (most common cutaneous manifestation of DM). Secondary to microangiopathy + neuropathy.Diagnose diabetes if not already known. Optimise glucose control. No specific treatment for dermopathy itself.
Spider Naevi / Palmar ErythemaSpider naevus: central arteriole + radiating vessels (>5 is pathological — a few are normal in pregnancy and children). Palmar erythema: erythema of thenar and hypothenar eminences. Leukonychia: pale nails from hypoalbuminaemia.Liver disease ★★★ (cirrhosis, hepatitis — ↑ oestrogen from reduced hepatic metabolism). Pregnancy (normal). Hyperthyroidism.Check LFTs + GGT + PT (liver synthetic function). Refer gastroenterology if >5 spider naevi or other signs of chronic liver disease.
Butterfly Rash (malar rash)Erythematous rash over cheeks and nose bridge — spares nasolabial folds (distinguishes from rosacea which affects nasolabial folds).SLE ★★★ (systemic lupus erythematosus). Also: rosacea (affects nasolabial folds), dermatomyositis (heliotrope rash), seborrhoeic dermatitis.ANA + anti-dsDNA + complement levels. Refer rheumatology if ANA positive. Photosensitivity is pathognomonic for SLE malar rash.
Pretibial MyxoedemaNon-pitting, brawny, yellow-orange plaque on anterior shins. Orange peel texture. Rare.Graves' disease ★★★ (hyperthyroidism — autoimmune). Thyroid dysfunction.Check TSH + TRAb (Graves' antibody). Refer endocrinology. Local corticosteroid intralesional or topical under occlusion.
